---
title: "Computer session 2"
format: 
  typst:
    number-sections: true
    include-before-body:
      - text: |
          // Gör alla länkar tydliga i PDF: blå + understrukna
          #show link: set text(fill: rgb("1a5fb4"))
          #show link: underline
execute: 
  eval: false
---

# Version control

This is a Git repo which you have now cloned. Read the instructions below and make the relevant changes to the relevant files.

::: callout-important
## Commit efter each subsection

After you have finished each section (starting from number 2), commit your changes by a message starting with the section number, for example `#2 first attempt`. You can make additional changes after that (with any informative commit message), but then label your first commit for section 3 with `3# bla bla bla` etc. You can use different branches etc for different sections of the instructions (if you would like to explore additional capabilities of gir, but it is not required for the course). Push your changes back to GitHub each time or more seldom if you prefer (but don't forget to do it at least when you finish!).
:::

Don't be afraid to break things. It is always possible to revert to a previous commit when things were still working!

# Targets

You need to have the targets and tarchetypes packages installed and loaded.

```{r}
if (!requireNamespace("targets", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("targets")
}
if (!requireNamespace("tarchetypes", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("tarchetypes")
}
library(targets)
```

Have a look at the `_targets.R` file and try to understand what will happen when you execute the pipeline for the first time by:

```{r}
tar_make()
```

You have now (hopefully) downloaded a zip file with all the data needed.

Lets unzip the zip file for easier access to the individual files it contains. Using targets, this should be performed by specifying a target in the `_targets.R` file.

The already defined target provides you with the filename to the zip-file (what is it?) in the object `zipdata.` Hint: use `tar_load(zipdata)` to load that object into your workspace and have a look + confirm that you understand where this information comes from (a certain call to `tar_target(_____)` as found in your code.

We will use the `zipdata` object as argument to the the unzip-function. Afer unzipping the folder, by `zip::unzip()` we also return the file names of all files put into the newly created `data-fixed` folder.

1.  Copy and paste the code below to the relevant place in the `_targets.R` file (be aware of trailing commas in the list!). Save the file (`Ctrl/Cmd + s`)
2.  Always include comments in your R code (`# bla bla bla ...`) to motivate what you do
3.  Run `tar_make()` from the console to execute your now updated pipeline .

```{r}
tar_target(csv_files, zip::unzip(zipdata))
```

Some data files (`.csv`) should now be found in the `data-fixed` folder (make sure that is the case).

## .gitignore

Make sure that no "sensitive" data will be shared on GitHub! (This particular data is synthetic but treat it as if it was real!)

# Data

The data previously unpacked is synthetic data from the [Synthea project](https://synthetichealth.github.io/synthea/).

::: callout-important
## Synthetic data

Note that no personal details are "real"! The data are all fake, alhough it aims to be realistic. As such, we can use it for learning purposes and we do not need to be afraid of sharing the data publically or transfering it between computers etc. Note, however, that the data can not be used for any "real" discovery of scientific importance! Hence, you might discover some interesting patterns or associations in the data which might be fictitious!
:::

## Preview the data

In Positron, you can simply click on the `.csv` files found in the `data-fixed/` folder to preview their contents. Play around with the filters and summaries to get a glance of the data you are given. [Help](https://positron.posit.co/data-explorer.html).

## fread

We need to read in the csv data before we can work with it in R. We like `{data.table}` objects when working with big data and can use `data.table::fread()` for individual csv files.

::: {.callout-note}
# duckplyr
Also feel free to explore the capabilities of `duckplyr::read_csv_duckdb()` as an alternative. It is sometimes more efficient (not required).
:::

We could also take all the csv file names from the `data-fixed` folder and make a big list of data.table objects. Try what happens if you run (and read the necessary function documentation to understand what you do) this code:

```{r}
fs::dir_map("data-fixed", data.table::fread)
```

When working within a pipeline it can be handy, however, to convert all csv data sets and cache them using a more efficient file format. We have instructed targets to use the qs2 storage format internally (its on one of the first lines in `_targets.R` although it is there called `qs` and not `qs2`; see [qs2](https://github.com/qsbase/qs2)).

In this case, however, we can use targets to not only read the files but also to store individual qs2-files for each original csv file. The code is a bit complex so it's already inserted in the pipeline.

1.  Uncomment the relevant section to activate the target using `tar_map()`.
2.  Try to understand what the uncommented code is supposed to do and add relevant comments to the code.
3.  Save and run `tar_make().`
4.  look in the `_targets/objects/` folder and confirm that your datasets have now been cached with prefix `dt_`
5.  Those files lack file extensions (they could have been named `dt_xxx.qs2` but lacks the `.qs2` part). Read one of the files by `tar_read(____)` where you replace `____` by the name of a dataset.

## Codebook/variable list

We now have access to a couple of data sets but honestly, we don't really know much about them yet!

Let's have a closer look at `dt_patients`. The data can be read as above but instead, let's load it by `tar_load(dt_patients)`. When loaded, tha data set will be available in your R session with the same name (`dt_patients`).

Perform your usual steps to familiarise yourself with this data set. Use functions such as `dim(), head(), dplyr::glimpse(), View(), summary(), skimr::skim()` etc.

But what do the columns actually mean? Some of them might be self evident and if you have some familiarity with the US you might guess for example that `ssn` means social security number (a different concept compared to the Swedish personal identity number). Here are some [descriptions of the data](https://github.com/synthetichealth/synthea/wiki/CSV-File-Data-Dictionary) (take a look!).

Have a look at the function `get_codebook()` (you'll find it in the R-folder). Add some comments to that file to describe what it's for. The best way to document a function is visualized in a [18 sec long screen recording](https://github.com/posit-dev/ark/pull/809). You might also have a closer look at [Documenting functions](https://roxygen2.r-lib.org/articles/rd.html) to additionaly structure your function documentation(not required but recommended).

Functions in the R-folder are accessed automatically from within the pipeline but to test those functions interactively, you must first explicitly load them by executing the function definition, source the containing file, or by running `tar_source()` (which is done in the beginning of `_taggets.R` and therefore implicitly when calling `tar_make()`)

Try running:

```{r}
tar_source()
get_codebook()
```

What is the result? What can it be used for? You need to understand at least some of the source code for the `get_codebook()` function to answer this!

Lets cache the output from this function by adding it to the targets pipeline. Add something like this at the relevant place in the `_targets.R` file:

```{r}
tar_target(codebook, _____())
```

Save the file and run `tar_make()`. Load the codebook and let's have a look at the variables found in the `dt_patients` data set:

```{r}
tar_load(codebook)
cb_patients <- codebook[file == "patients.csv"]

# Which columns are relevant for `patients`?
cb_patients[, column_name]

# Well, those columns don't actually match the column names used in the data!
# Do they?
names(dt_patients)
# Do you notice a pattern in how the names differ?
# Let's fix that
cb_patients[, column_name := tolower(column_name)]

# Do we have any variables which are not documented?
setdiff(names(dt_patients), cb_patients[, column_name]) # Non-documented

# Yep, so let's fix that manually for now
cb_patients[, column_name := gsub(" county code", "", column_name)]
```

OK, well we found somethings that we might need to change in order to match the actual variable names to the codebook. We did it interactively above but we need to do this in a way so that the targets pipeline will get it right as well! It actually seems that all our data sets have only lower case column names even if that is not the case according to the formal documentation found online.

1.  Modify the `get_codebook()` function so it will change all variable names to lower case (inserting `tolower()` somewhere).
2.  save and rerun the pipeline (`tar_make()`)

::: {.callout-tip}
# Standardized variable names
Variable/column names from raw data can be very messy (including spaces, non-latin characters, starting with a number etc). One way to standardize the names is by `janitor::clean_names()` ([documentation](https://sfirke.github.io/janitor/reference/clean_names.html)) but for now our intent is not to make the names as standardized as possible, but rather to make them comparable to their corresponding names from the variable list.
:::

Are there any additional columns from other datasets which are not described correctly by the codebook?

Try to understand what I do with the following code, run it yourself, interpret the result and draw a conclusin how the observed vs defined variable names differ:

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(data.table)

names_in_data <-
  tibble(files = dir("_targets/objects", "dt_")) |>
  mutate(
    column_name = map(files, \(x) names(tar_read_raw(x)), .progress = TRUE),
    file = paste0(gsub("dt_", "", files), ".csv")
  ) |>
  unnest(column_name) |>
  setDT()

codebook[, column_name := tolower(column_name)]

names_in_data[!codebook, on = c("file", "column_name")]
codebook[!names_in_data, on = c("file", "column_name")] |> View()
```

Well, such missmatches can be very frustrating and it often takes a lot of time to fix! Unfortunately, this is the reality in many real world cases! The interpretation of a data set is selldom as intuitive as one might hope. But let's not get stuck here for the moment!

## Labels

Codebooks/variable list are somewhat helpful but they usually exist as a separate document (a PDF, or web page as illustrated above). We showed how to convert the documentation to something more useful above but wouldn't it be nice to integrate it more directly with the data? People working with SPSS data are quite used to having labels attached to their column names. This is not the default in R but the [package labelled](https://larmarange.github.io/labelled/) can be used (install it!).

Add labels to the patient data:

```{r}
labelled::var_label(dt_patients) <-
  cb_patients[, setNames(description, column_name)]
```

Now, what does ssn actually mean?

```{r}
labelled::look_for(dt_patients, "ssn")
```

What does income mean?

```{r}
labelled::look_for(dt_patients, "income")
```

Well that's some level of detail but is it enough for analysis? Does this include all sorts of income? Salary, insurance, benefits, pensions etc? What is the unit of measurement? SEK? DKK? USD? Does the value need to be multiplied by 1000 to get the actual value? Is it income from the last known year, an averager over time or from some other year?

```{r}
dt_patients[, hist(income)]
```

Woo, we have some rich people here! Who are they? Where can I find them? Let me check their adresses ... WAIT, WHAT?

One package using labels is [{gtsummary}](https://www.danieldsjoberg.com/gtsummary/). Lets make a simple table 1 (a demography table, generally the first table to appear in a scientific article in medicine/epidemiology) for our cohort.

Note that the date format (`IDate` from `data.table` package) is not recognized by gtsummary (feel free to [submit a "bug report"/issue](https://github.com/ddsjoberg/gtsummary/issues) if you prefer). Dates and times are often tricky! In this case we might first convert tha date columns to R:s native date format (instead of the more efficient IDate format as used by data.table).

But, let's ignore the dates for now and just pick a few other variables which might seem relevant:

::: {.callout-note}
# What do the labels actually mean?

- `race`: In medical and epidemiological research, it is not uncommon to encounter a variable labelled `race`, particularly in data originating from certain countries. For Swedish students, this may appear unusual, as such categorisation is generally not used in Swedish registers. The meaning, construction, and legal basis of this variable vary substantially across countries, and it may reflect self-identification, administrative classification, or other criteria.
- `marital status`: The definition and classification of marital status may differ across countries and historical periods. For example, marriage is currently gender-neutral in Sweden. However, when working with historical data, categories such as “registered partnership” (previously used for same-sex couples) may need to be reclassified depending on the analytical purpose. Legal definitions may also change over time.
- `gender`: In Swedish population registers, this variable is typically derived from the personal identity number, where the parity (odd/even) of a specific digit indicates legal sex. More precisely, this reflects legal sex, which may or may not correspond to biological sex or gender identity. Careful terminology is therefore important when interpreting such variables.
- `country of birth` / `citizenship`: Such variables are often treated as interchangeable, but they represent different concepts. Country of birth is typically fixed over time, whereas citizenship may change (and one may have multiple citizenships). In addition, citizenship does not necessarily reflect migration background, ethnicity, or cultural identity. Historical changes in state borders may also affect interpretation: for example, a person recorded as born in the Soviet Union may today be associated with a country that did not exist at the time of birth. When analysing such variables, it is therefore important to understand both the administrative definition used in the register and the geopolitical context at the time of registration.
:::

```{r}
gtsummary::tbl_summary(dt_patients[, .(
  marital,
  race,
  ethnicity,
  gender,
  state
)])
```

Note that we have informative labels for the different column names but we are still lacking interpratable labels for individual factor levels.

What's your conclusion based on the data? Do we have data from the whole US population? Is it a representative sample from the whole country (based on the variables we choosed to include in the table, age, social status and a myriad other factors currently ignored)?

## Data scans

Have a look at the [pointblank](https://rstudio.github.io/pointblank/) package.

Install the package and look at the documentation for `?scan_data`

Let's summarise whats in the `dt_patients` data:

```{r}
dt_patients |> pointblank::scan_data(sections = "OMSV")
```

See, the nice labels are used here to :-)

Wouldn't it be nice to have such documentation saved for all the data sets we are working with?

Look at the `export_data_scan()` function found in the R-folder and try to grasp how it works and add some comments to it (use the Roxygen2 sceleton as demonstrated in the short video above if you like).

Add something like this to the targets pipeline (what does `format = "file"` mean? What is the output from the `export_data_scan()`). Hint: there is one function name and one argument to that function missing below.

```{r}
tar_target(data_scan_patient, ___________(______, "patients"), format = "file")
```

Add multiple targets for all data sets which seem relevant and look at the output to get to know your data better!

There are better ways to generate data_scans for all data sets (instead of repeating the same line for each data set of choice, but it's more complicated. The [documentation](https://books.ropensci.org/targets/) might help if you want to try it on your own).

::: {.callout-note}
# `max_rows`
`export_data_scan()` has an argument `max_rows` with a default value. What does it mean? There seems to be no good reason for it and you can try to choose a higher number or to the remove the limitation it is imposing.
It is currently a work around for [this reported issue](https://github.com/rstudio/pointblank/issues/550).
If you agree that this is a problem, please feel free to upvote Hadleys comment in the beginning of the issue.
:::

## Keys

The data originates from a database where the concept of primary and foreign keys exists. This could be seen by the key column from the codebook. The primary key is a unique identifier for each row in a dataset. Which ones do we have?

```{r}
tar_read(codebook)[key == "primary", .(file, column_name)]
```

Let's set the key for each of the datasets where this is relevant.

Add the id key to dt_patients by:

```{r}
setkey(dt_patients, id)
```

We could do this by adding new targets to the pipeline. If so, however, we would end up with multiple versions of each cached data set. It seems unnecessary. Let's instead go back and add the keys aready before they are cached the first time. You were previsously asked to uncomment a line including the `fread()` function. We could add the key argument here to use the id column as key. The problem is that not all data sets have such id columns so you would need something extra. Try to found a solution if you have time and interest (otherwise not). 

Tip: The current function code involves a call to: `fread(path)`. You could make a new function, maybe something like 

```{r}
#| eval: false
fread_key <- function(path) {
  x <- fread(path)
  if (hasName(x, "Id")) {
    setkey(x, Id)
  }
  x
}
```

Save this function in a script under the `R/folder` and replace tha current `fread(path)` call in `_targets.R` with `fread_key(path)` and then rerun the pipeline by `tar_make()`.

# Commit and push

Don’t forget to push your changes to GitHub after your final commit.  
Then follow the submission instructions provided in the Canvas assignment.

If you were not able to complete certain tasks above (which can happen even to experienced users), this may be due to technical circumstances beyond your control — for example, unexpected package updates, system-specific settings, or version differences between environments.

In such cases, please create a file called `COMMENTS.md` in your project folder and:

- briefly explain what did not work,
- describe any error messages you encountered,
- outline what you tried to do,
- and reflect on possible reasons and potential solutions.

Clear documentation of problems and reasoning is part of good computational practice.
